PIMP Q: What nerves are at risk in triangle of pain? A: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and femoral branch of genitofemoral
PIMP Q: What is the advantage of robotic eTEP? A: Enhanced precision in confined preperitoneal space
PIMP Q: What mesh overlap is required? A: 5cm beyond all hernia defects

name: Open Shouldice Repair
overview: Tension-free tissue-only repair of inguinal hernia
indications: Primary inguinal hernia in young patients
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the Shouldice technique? A: Four-layer reinforcement of posterior wall without mesh
PIMP Q: What is the recurrence rate? A: <1% in expert hands, 5-10% in general practice
PIMP Q: What patients are ideal candidates? A: Young, active patients with primary hernias and good tissue
PIMP Q: Why avoid tension? A: Tension causes pain, recurrence, and tissue ischemia

name: Sports Hernia Repair (Athletic Pubalgia)
overview: Repair of chronic groin pain in athletes without obvious hernia
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is athletic pubalgia? A: Weakness or tear in muscles/tendons around pubic area causing groin pain
PIMP Q: What imaging is helpful? A: MRI showing posterior wall deficiency or adductor pathology
PIMP Q: What is the return to sport rate? A: 85-95% return to previous level with proper repair
PIMP Q: What conservative measures should be tried? A: Rest, physical therapy, steroid injection for 6-12 weeks

name: Parastomal Hernia Repair
overview: Repair of hernia around ostomy site
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the incidence of parastomal hernia? A: 30-50% of patients develop parastomal hernia
PIMP Q: What mesh type should be used? A: Biologic or composite mesh to minimize erosion risk
PIMP Q: What is the most effective technique? A: Prophylactic mesh placement at initial stoma creation
PIMP Q: When should stoma be relocated? A: Very large hernias or failed previous repairs

name: Open Hiatal Hernia Repair
overview: Open surgical repair of large hiatal hernia
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What defines a large hiatal hernia? A: >5cm or >30% of stomach in chest
PIMP Q: When is Collis gastroplasty needed? A: Shortened esophagus preventing tension-free repair
PIMP Q: What mesh can be used for reinforcement? A: Biologic mesh to avoid erosion into esophagus
PIMP Q: What is the recurrence rate? A: 10-42% depending on size and repair technique

name: Robotic Hiatal Hernia Repair
overview: Robot-assisted repair of hiatal hernia with fundoplication
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the advantage of robotic hiatal repair? A: Enhanced suturing precision for crural repair and fundoplication
PIMP Q: What wristed instruments provide benefit? A: Precise intracorporeal suturing in confined space
PIMP Q: How do you prevent recurrence? A: Adequate mobilization, tension-free repair, and secure crural repair
PIMP Q: What fundoplication type is preferred? A: Nissen (360°) or Toupet (270°) depending on motility

name: Wide Local Excision (Melanoma)
overview: Wide surgical excision of melanoma with appropriate margins
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What margins are required for melanoma? A: 0.5cm for in situ, 1cm for <1mm, 2cm for >1mm thickness
PIMP Q: When is sentinel lymph node biopsy indicated? A: Melanoma >1mm thick or high-risk features
PIMP Q: What is the most important prognostic factor? A: Breslow thickness (depth of invasion)
PIMP Q: How should specimens be oriented? A: Mark with sutures or ink for pathologist orientation

name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (Melanoma)
overview: Minimally invasive lymph node staging for melanoma
positioning: Depends on primary site and node basin
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the false negative rate of SLNB? A: 5-10% when performed correctly
PIMP Q: What defines a positive sentinel node? A: Any melanoma cells detected by H&E or immunohistochemistry
PIMP Q: What tracer combination is preferred? A: Technetium-99m sulfur colloid and blue dye
PIMP Q: What is the survival benefit of SLNB? A: Improved staging but no overall survival benefit

name: Rotational Flap Closure
overview: Reconstruction using rotational skin flap for wound coverage
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the ideal flap to defect ratio? A: 3:1 or 4:1 flap length to defect width
PIMP Q: How do you assess flap viability? A: Color, capillary refill, temperature, and bleeding
PIMP Q: What causes flap necrosis? A: Insufficient blood supply, excessive tension, or infection
PIMP Q: How do you minimize dog-ears? A: Proper flap design and strategic tissue excision

name: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (Wound VAC)
overview: Application of controlled negative pressure for wound healing
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What pressure settings are typically used? A: 75-125 mmHg continuous or intermittent
PIMP Q: How often should dressing changes occur? A: Every 48-72 hours or per protocol
PIMP Q: What are contraindications to VAC therapy? A: Exposed vessels, anastomoses, organs, or untreated osteomyelitis
PIMP Q: What promotes wound healing with VAC? A: Removes fluid, reduces edema, promotes granulation

name: Lipoma Excision
overview: Surgical removal of benign fatty tumors
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most common soft tissue tumor? A: Lipoma - benign fatty tumor
PIMP Q: When should you be concerned about malignancy? A: Rapid growth, pain, size >5cm, or deep location
PIMP Q: What is the recurrence rate after complete excision? A: <5% with intact capsule removal
PIMP Q: What imaging is helpful for deep lipomas? A: MRI to assess relationship to neurovascular structures

name: Sebaceous Cyst Excision
overview: Surgical removal of epidermoid or sebaceous cysts
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the key to preventing recurrence? A: Complete removal of intact cyst wall
PIMP Q: Should infected cysts be excised? A: Treat infection first, then excise when inflammation resolves
PIMP Q: What is the difference between epidermoid and sebaceous cysts? A: Epidermoid cysts contain keratin, sebaceous cysts contain sebum
PIMP Q: What is pilonidal cyst? A: Specific type of cyst in natal cleft area requiring specialized treatment

name: Total Pelvic Exenteration
overview: Complete removal of pelvic organs for advanced malignancy
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the 5-year survival after pelvic exenteration? A: 40-60% for selected patients with clear margins
PIMP Q: What diversions are created? A: Ileal conduit for urine, colostomy for feces
PIMP Q: What are the main contraindications? A: Distant metastases, pelvic sidewall involvement, medical unfitness
PIMP Q: What reconstruction is needed? A: Pelvic floor, urinary diversion, intestinal continuity

name: Cytoreductive Surgery with HIPEC
overview: Maximal tumor debulking followed by heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy
positioning: Supine with access to entire abdomen
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What temperature is used for HIPEC? A: 41-43°C for 60-90 minutes
PIMP Q: What defines complete cytoreduction? A: No visible tumor deposits or residual <2.5mm
PIMP Q: What chemotherapy agents are used? A: Mitomycin C, cisplatin, or oxaliplatin depending on tumor type
PIMP Q: What is the survival benefit? A: Can extend median survival to 22-63 months in selected patients

name: Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt)
overview: Division of pyloric muscle for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the classic presentation of pyloric stenosis? A: 2-8 week old male with projectile non-bilious vomiting
PIMP Q: What electrolyte abnormality is expected? A: Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
PIMP Q: What ultrasound findings confirm diagnosis? A: Pyloric muscle >3mm thick and >15mm long
PIMP Q: What is the key to successful myotomy? A: Complete division of muscle without mucosal injury

name: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia Repair
overview: Surgical repair of congenital diaphragmatic defect
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most common type of CDH? A: Left posterolateral (Bochdalek) hernia 85%
PIMP Q: What determines survival in CDH? A: Degree of pulmonary hypoplasia and pulmonary hypertension
PIMP Q: When should repair be performed? A: After hemodynamic stabilization, not as emergency
PIMP Q: What patch material is used for large defects? A: Gore-Tex or other non-absorbable synthetic material

name: Hirschsprung Pull-Through
overview: Surgical treatment for Hirschsprung disease with aganglionic bowel
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is Hirschsprung disease? A: Congenital absence of enteric ganglia in distal bowel
PIMP Q: What procedure is most commonly performed? A: Transanal endorectal pull-through (TEPT)
PIMP Q: How do you confirm adequate resection? A: Frozen section showing ganglion cells
PIMP Q: What is the most serious late complication? A: Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC)

name: Undescended Testis (Orchiopexy)
overview: Surgical repositioning of undescended testis into scrotum
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the optimal age for orchiopexy? A: 6-12 months to preserve fertility potential
PIMP Q: What is the cancer risk in undescended testis? A: 5-10 fold increased risk of testicular cancer
PIMP Q: When is orchiectomy considered? A: Non-viable testis or post-pubertal unilateral cryptorchidism
PIMP Q: What is a vanishing testis? A: Absent testis due to in utero torsion or agenesis

name: Paracentesis
overview: Percutaneous drainage of peritoneal fluid
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: Where is the safest site for paracentesis? A: Left lower quadrant lateral to rectus sheath
PIMP Q: How much fluid can be safely removed? A: <5L in single session to avoid complications
PIMP Q: What lab studies are needed on ascitic fluid? A: Cell count, albumin, protein, culture
PIMP Q: What is the SAAG calculation? A: Serum-ascites albumin gradient = serum albumin - ascites albumin

name: Thoracentesis
overview: Percutaneous drainage of pleural fluid
positioning: Sitting leaning forward or lateral decubitus
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: Where should the needle be inserted? A: Above the rib to avoid intercostal vessels underneath
PIMP Q: How much fluid can be safely removed? A: <1.5L in single session
PIMP Q: What are Light's criteria? A: LDH >2/3 upper limit, protein >3g/dL, pleural/serum LDH >0.6
PIMP Q: When should chest tube be placed instead? A: Empyema, large volume, or recurrent effusion

name: Lumbar Puncture
overview: Percutaneous access to cerebrospinal fluid
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What anatomic landmark identifies L4? A: Intercristal line connecting iliac crests
PIMP Q: What CSF pressure is normal? A: 10-18 cm H2O in lateral position
PIMP Q: How do you differentiate SAH from traumatic tap? A: Xanthochromia in SAH, clearing tubes in traumatic tap
PIMP Q: What needle size minimizes headache risk? A: Small gauge (22G) atraumatic needle

name: Transvenous Pacemaker Placement
overview: Temporary cardiac pacing via central venous access
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What are the signs of good lead position? A: Good capture at <1mA, sensing R waves, stable position
PIMP Q: Where should the lead tip be positioned? A: Right ventricular apex for stability
PIMP Q: What is the most serious complication? A: Cardiac perforation leading to tamponade
PIMP Q: When should permanent pacing be considered? A: Persistent conduction abnormalities after 5-7 days

name: Myomectomy (Open)
overview: Open surgical removal of uterine fibroids preserving uterus
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the recurrence rate after myomectomy? A: 15-30% develop new fibroids within 5 years
PIMP Q: When should cesarean delivery be planned? A: If endometrial cavity was entered during myomectomy
PIMP Q: What medication can shrink fibroids preoperatively? A: GnRH agonists reduce size by 30-50%
PIMP Q: What is the most serious intraoperative complication? A: Hemorrhage requiring emergency hysterectomy (1-2%)

name: D&C (Dilation and Curettage)
overview: Cervical dilation and uterine curettage for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most serious complication of D&C? A: Uterine perforation with potential bowel injury
PIMP Q: What is Asherman syndrome? A: Intrauterine adhesions from excessive curettage
PIMP Q: When should hysteroscopy be added? A: Focal lesions or when direct visualization needed
PIMP Q: What anesthesia is typically used? A: IV sedation or general anesthesia for patient comfort

name: Vulvectomy
overview: Surgical removal of vulvar tissue for malignancy
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What margins are required for vulvar cancer? A: 1-2cm margins depending on tumor characteristics
PIMP Q: When is sentinel lymph node biopsy appropriate? A: Early stage disease with tumor <4cm
PIMP Q: What is the most common histology? A: Squamous cell carcinoma (85-90%)
PIMP Q: What reconstruction options exist? A: Advancement flaps, rotational flaps, or skin grafts

name: Cervical Cerclage
overview: Cervical reinforcement procedure to prevent preterm birth
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the ideal timing for cerclage placement? A: 12-14 weeks gestation for prophylactic cerclage
PIMP Q: What suture material is preferred? A: Non-absorbable suture like Mersilene or Prolene
PIMP Q: When should cerclage be removed? A: 36-37 weeks or onset of labor
PIMP Q: What is the success rate? A: 80-90% pregnancy prolongation to term

name: VATS Sympathectomy
overview: Video-assisted thoracoscopic sympathetic chain division
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most common complication? A: Compensatory hyperhidrosis in trunk/legs 85% of patients
PIMP Q: How do you prevent Horner syndrome? A: Avoid injury to stellate ganglion (T1)
PIMP Q: What level should be divided for palmar hyperhidrosis? A: T2-T3 ganglia
PIMP Q: What is gustatory sweating? A: Facial sweating triggered by eating or taste

name: Nuss Procedure (Pectus Excavatum)
overview: Minimally invasive repair of pectus excavatum deformity
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the Haller index? A: CT measurement of chest width/depth ratio, >3.25 is severe
PIMP Q: What age is optimal for Nuss procedure? A: 8-14 years when chest wall is most malleable
PIMP Q: How long does the bar stay in place? A: 2-4 years to allow chest wall remodeling
PIMP Q: What is the most serious complication? A: Cardiac injury during substernal dissection

name: Chest Wall Resection
overview: En bloc resection of chest wall for tumor or infection
indications: Lung cancer with chest wall invasion
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: How many ribs can be resected without reconstruction? A: 3-4 posterior ribs, 2-3 lateral ribs, 1-2 anterior ribs
PIMP Q: What materials are used for reconstruction? A: Mesh (Gore-Tex, Marlex) with or without methylmethacrylate
PIMP Q: When is muscle flap coverage needed? A: Large defects or when mesh is exposed
PIMP Q: What is the mortality rate? A: 5-15% depending on extent of resection

name: Diaphragmatic Plication
overview: Surgical reinforcement of paralyzed or weakened diaphragm
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What test confirms diaphragmatic paralysis? A: Fluoroscopic sniff test showing paradoxical movement
PIMP Q: When should plication be performed? A: Symptomatic patients with confirmed paralysis >6 months
PIMP Q: What suture technique is preferred? A: Non-absorbable interrupted sutures in horizontal mattress fashion
PIMP Q: What is the success rate? A: 80-90% improvement in symptoms and pulmonary function

name: Lung Volume Reduction Surgery
overview: Surgical resection of emphysematous lung tissue
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What CT pattern predicts best outcome? A: Heterogeneous upper lobe emphysema with lower lobe compression
PIMP Q: What is the mortality from LVRS? A: 5-10% operative mortality
PIMP Q: What functional improvement is expected? A: 20-30% improvement in FEV1 and exercise capacity
PIMP Q: How long do benefits last? A: Greatest improvement in first 2 years, then gradual decline

name: VATS Pericardial Window
overview: Minimally invasive drainage of pericardial effusion
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What size window is created? A: 4-6cm pericardial resection for adequate drainage
PIMP Q: Where is the phrenic nerve located? A: Runs anterior to hilum, must be identified and preserved
PIMP Q: What is the advantage over subxiphoid approach? A: Better visualization and larger window possible
PIMP Q: What is the recurrence rate? A: <5% with adequate window size

name: Kidney Transplant (Recipient)
overview: Renal transplantation for end-stage kidney disease
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the preferred vascular anastomosis? A: End-to-side to external iliac vessels
PIMP Q: What immunosuppression is used? A: Triple therapy: tacrolimus, mycophenolate, prednisone
PIMP Q: What is the 5-year graft survival? A: >85% for living donor, >75% for deceased donor
PIMP Q: What causes early graft loss? A: Acute tubular necrosis, rejection, vascular thrombosis

name: Liver Transplant (Recipient Overview)
overview: Orthotopic liver transplantation for end-stage liver disease
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most serious early complication? A: Hepatic artery thrombosis leading to graft loss
PIMP Q: What biliary reconstruction is preferred? A: Duct-to-duct anastomosis over Roux-en-Y
PIMP Q: What is the 5-year survival rate? A: 70-80% overall survival
PIMP Q: What defines primary non-function? A: Severe graft dysfunction requiring retransplantation within 7 days

name: Living Donor Nephrectomy
overview: Laparoscopic kidney harvest from living donor
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: Why is left kidney preferred? A: Longer renal vein facilitates recipient surgery
PIMP Q: What is acceptable warm ischemia time? A: <2-3 minutes for optimal graft function
PIMP Q: What is the donor mortality risk? A: 1 in 3000 (0.03%) perioperative mortality
PIMP Q: What long-term risk exists for donors? A: Slightly increased risk of hypertension and proteinuria

name: Intraosseous Access
overview: Emergency vascular access through bone marrow
pearlsHighYield: PIMP Q: What is the most common insertion site? A: Proximal tibia, 2cm below and medial to tibial tuberosity
PIMP Q: What flow rates are achievable? A: 100-200 mL/hr by gravity, faster with pressure
PIMP Q: What medications can be given IO? A: All IV medications and fluids
PIMP Q: How do you confirm proper placement? A: Aspiration of marrow and easy infusion without resistance
